segments.p2-7-g1: "Э" is a short exclamatory interjection used to call attention or express mild surprise; here it begins the spoken remark and has no grammatical ties to the rest of the sentence.
segments.p2-7-g2: "полно" is used as an interjection meaning "enough" or "come now" to dismiss or shut down an idea; it functions independently here to cut off further discussion rather than as a literal quantity.
segments.p2-7-g3: The proper name "Лиза" is in the vocative/addressing position, simply naming the person spoken to; punctuation (comma) marks direct address.
segments.p2-7-g4: "какая уж тут книга" is an idiomatic, dismissive phrase: "какая (уж) тут" weakens the noun that follows to mean "what (kind of) ... is there (now)"; "тут" here means "in this situation/at this point," and "книга" is the noun being dismissed.
segments.p2-7-g5: "когда" introduces the subordinate clause "when ..."; the impersonal feeling construction uses dative "мне" plus the adverbial predicate "гадко" (it is disgusting to me), intensified by "самому" (meaning "to me myself"), and modified by the adverb "вчуже" (archaic: "as if a stranger / feeling out of place").
segments.p2-7-g6: "Да и" here works together as a colloquial connective meaning "besides/and besides" or "moreover," then the negation "не" applies to the adverb "вчуже" ("as a stranger"); the whole short sentence denies the characterization just suggested.
segments.p2-7-g7: "У меня" is the possessive/experiential construction meaning "in me" or "for me"; "все это" (neuter singular idea) is the grammatical subject; "теперь" is an adverb modifying the verb; "в душе" is a locative phrase "in (my) soul/heart"; "проснулось" is past-tense, neuter, perfective reflexive of "проснуться" agreeing with the neuter subject "все это" (literally, "has awakened").
segments.p2-7-g10: "Неужели" is a rhetorical exclamatory particle meaning "really?/is it possible?" used to express surprise or incredulity; the comma shows a pause before the following restatement.
segments.p2-7-g11: The repeated "неужели" intensifies disbelief; "тебе" is the dative pronoun "to you" used with the impersonal predicate "гадко" ("it is disgusting to [you]"); "самой" is an emphatic/reflexive dative form "yourself" (feminine) stressing that it is the addressee personally; "не" negates "гадко"; "здесь" is a locative adverb "here." Together they form the question "Don't you yourself find it disgusting here?"
segments.p2-7-g52: А is a contrastive/conjunctive particle here ('and'/'but') starting the sentence; здесь is a locative adverb meaning 'here' in the present situation; я is the 1st-person subject; ведь is an emphatic particle meaning 'after all'/'you know' that strengthens the assertion; знаю is the 1st-person singular present of знать ('I know'/'I'm aware') and introduces the content clause that follows.
segments.p2-7-g53: что is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the reported content; я is the subject repeated for emphasis; только is the adverb 'only/just' (here: 'merely'); свистни is a colloquial perfective imperative (2nd person singular) meaning 'whistle' or 'give a whistle' — the whole phrase functions like a conditional/result: 'that I only whistle (and ...).'
segments.p2-7-g54: и is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the result clause; ты is the 2nd-person singular pronoun 'you', here the subject of the following parenthetical phrase.
segments.p2-7-g55: хочешь не хочешь is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'whether you want to or not'; each хочешь is the present indicative 2nd-person singular 'you want', with the negative particle не between them to form the fixed phrase.
segments.p2-7-g56: иди is the imperative (2nd-person singular) of идти, here 'go' used idiomatically as 'follow'; за plus the instrumental мной (за мной) literally 'after/behind me' means 'follow me' or 'come after me'.
segments.p2-7-g57: и is 'and' continuing the thought; уж is an intensifying particle ('already'/'indeed') adding force; не negates the clause; я is the subject; с + твоей волей is an instrumental phrase 'by/with your will' (i.e. 'in relation to your will'); спрашиваюсь is the reflexive present 1st-person form meaning 'I am asked/held to account' or 'I am the one who is questioned' — together: 'and I am no longer the one to be governed/answered to by your will.'
segments.p2-7-g58: а is the contrasting conjunction 'but'; ты is 'you'; с моей is an elliptical instrumental phrase ('with mine') where the noun (волей) is omitted but understood — the contrast means 'but you [will answer] to my will.'
segments.p2-7-g59: «Последний мужик» is the subject phrase meaning ‘the very last/most humble peasant.’ «Наймется» is a reflexive future (he will hire himself out); «в работники» is a role/position phrase with в + accusative meaning ‘as a worker.’ «Все-таки» (here split into two tokens) is an adverbial qualifier ‘still/after all,’ and the clause ends with the negative phrase «не всего себя закабалит» where «не» negates the perfective future «закабалит» (‘will enslave’) and «всего себя» = ‘his whole self’ (object). The line means he will take hired work but will not completely enslave himself.
segments.p2-7-g60: «Да и» here functions as a colloquial connective meaning ‘and besides/moreover’; «знает» is 3rd-person singular present ‘he knows’ and refers back to the peasant subject.
segments.p2-7-g61: This is a subordinate clause introduced by «что» = ‘that’; «ему» is dative ‘to/for him’ (experiencer), «срок» is the noun ‘term/deadline/period,’ and «есть» is the present existential/linking verb ‘there is / is.’ Together: ‘that for him there is a term’ (i.e. he has a limit/term).
segments.p2-7-g62: «А» is a contrasting/connecting particle here (‘and/so’); «где» is the interrogative adverb ‘where’; «твой» is the possessive ‘your’ modifying «срок»; Russian omits a copula in the present, so the question asks ‘Where is your term/limit?’
segments.p2-7-g63: «Подумай» is the singular imperative ‘think/consider’; «только» here is a colloquial particle meaning ‘just/only’ that softens or emphasizes the command.
segments.p2-7-g64: «Что» introduces the direct question ‘what’; «ты» is the subject ‘you’; «здесь» is the place adverb ‘here’ (in this situation); «отдаешь» is present tense ‘you give’ and in this context means ‘give up/surrender’ (what are you surrendering?).
segments.p2-7-g100: Не is the sentential negation particle (“not”). Верю is the 1st-person singular present of верить; the subject (я) is omitted as is normal in Russian, so the short two-word sentence means “I don’t believe (it/that).” It’s an elliptical, emphatic denial.
segments.p2-7-g101: Как here means “how” (in what way / how could); он is the 3rd-person masculine pronoun “he.” Будет is the 3rd-person singular of быть used as the future auxiliary (“will”) + the infinitive любить to form the simple future (“will love”). The object (тебя) appears later, so word order postpones the object for emphasis.
segments.p2-7-g102: Коли is an archaic/colloquial conjunction meaning “if/when” (here conditional: “if”). Знает is 3rd-person singular present of знать, “he knows” (introducing the condition under which the previous clause holds).
segments.p2-7-g103: Что introduces a subordinate content clause (“that”). Тебя is the accusative object “you.” От + него literally “from him,” indicating movement away. Сейчас is an adverb “now/this very instant.” Кликнут is 3rd-person plural perfective (they will call/summon) with an implicit indefinite subject (“they”/people); used impersonally with тебя as the accusative patient: “you will be called/summoned away.”
segments.p2-7-g104: Пакостник is a nominative noun used as an exclamation, “a mischief-maker / nasty fellow.” Он follows for emphasis (“he is”). После этого is a temporal/causal phrase “after that / because of that,” referring to the preceding action; the whole line is an emphatic denunciation.
segments.p2-7-g105: Уважает is the 3rd-person singular present of уважать (“respects”). Ли is the enclitic question particle that turns the clause into a yes/no question. Он is the subject “he,” тебя the accusative object “you.” Хоть here means “even / at least,” and на каплю is a measure expression literally “by a drop,” idiomatically “even a bit / the slightest amount.”
segments.p2-7-g65: "что" is the interrogative 'what' asking for the object of the verb; "кабалишь" is 2nd person singular present (imperfective) of the colloquial/archaic verb 'кабалить' meaning 'to subject to bondage/enslave'. Together this is a blunt rhetorical question: 'What are you enslaving?'.
segments.p2-7-g66: "Душу" is the accusative singular of 'душа' (direct object). The single-word segment as an exclamation repeats and foregrounds the object: 'the soul'.
segments.p2-7-g67: A second repetition of the accusative "душу" for emphasis; stylistically emphatic and appositive to the following relative clause.
segments.p2-7-g68: "в которой" is a relative phrase ('in which') modifying душу; "ты" is the 2nd-person singular subject; "невластна" is a short-form adjective (feminine singular) used predicatively meaning 'are not in power / are powerless over'. The feminine short form signals the addressee is viewed as feminine and yields 'the soul in which you have no power.'
segments.p2-7-g69: "кабалишь" (2nd person singular present) repeats the verb 'enslave'; "вместе с" plus the instrumental "телом" means 'together with the body' (вместе с + instr. = 'along with'). Grammatically the verb takes the repeated accusative 'душу' as object and the phrase 'вместе с телом' as an accompanying/adverbial phrase.
segments.p2-7-g70: "Любовь свою" is the accusative object with the possessive 'свою' agreeing in gender and number ('your love'). "на поругание" is 'for/to the purpose of insult' (на + accusative = indicating result/purpose). "всякому пьянице" is dative, marking the person who will be the agent/recipient of the mockery ('by/for every drunkard'). "отдаешь" is 2nd person singular present of 'отдавать' used here in the sense 'surrender/give away' (you give up/surrender). Together: 'You give up your love for the mockery of any drunkard.'
segments.p2-7-g71: A one-word nominative exclamation. The word is foregrounded as an emphatic interjection: 'Love!' (repeating/echoing the previous sentence's theme).
segments.p2-7-g106: Что is the interrogative pronoun 'what' introducing the question. У тебя is a possessive locative construction literally 'at you' used to indicate 'do you have' (possession/relationship). С ним is a prepositional phrase 'with him' (с + instrumental), and общего is the adjective общие used substantively ('common') — together the structure asks 'what common [thing] do you have with him?'. Word order is idiomatic: Что + у тебя + с ним + общего? = 'What do you have in common with him?'.
segments.p2-7-g107: Смеется (3rd person singular present imperfective) means 'he laughs' or 'mocks'; the subject он follows the verb for emphasis. Над тобой is 'at/over you' (nad + instrumental) indicating the target of the mockery. Да here functions as an old-fashioned coordinating 'and' (joining the clauses), and the doubled object (тебя же) with же is emphatic ('you, of all people') before the present imperfective обкрадывает 'robs/cheats' (habitual or repeated action). Вот и вся его любовь is an idiomatic summation: 'that is all his love' (вот и = 'that's/there you have it').
segments.p2-7-g108: Хорошо is an impersonal predicative ('it's good' / 'it's a good thing'); ещё modifies it here to add the sense 'still/at least' or 'all the better'. This whole phrase sets up a comment of relief.
segments.p2-7-g109: Что is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content clause. Не бьет is 3rd person singular present negative 'doesn't beat/strike' (the subject 'he' is understood from context, and the object 'you' is implied from the previous sentence).
segments.p2-7-g110: А is a contrastive conjunction ('and/but') starting a turn; может is from мочь used impersonally here to mean 'maybe'/'it may be' (possibility). Together А может = 'or maybe'.
segments.p2-7-g111: И is linking/emphatic here ('even' / 'also') tied to the previous hedge 'maybe', and бьет is 3rd person singular present 'beats/strikes' (the object 'you' remains implied). The short clause expresses the possibility that he actually does hit the person.
segments.p2-7-g84: The phrase 'Да ведь' is an emphatic particle pair meaning 'after all' or 'you see'. The clause uses an existential negation with 'нет' at the end: 'обиды сильнее ... нет' literally 'there is no insult stronger' — i.e. 'there is nothing more hurtful'. Word order places the noun and comparative before 'нет' for emphasis.
segments.p2-7-g85: 'Понимаешь ли ты?' is a simple present-tense question with the enclitic 'ли' turning the statement into a yes/no question; the verb precedes the subject here (verb–subject order) for more colloquial emphasis: 'do you understand?'
segments.p2-7-g86: 'Вот' here is an interjection meaning 'well' or 'you see', used to call attention before the remark.
segments.p2-7-g87: 'Слышал я' is past-tense, first-person ('I heard' / 'I've heard'); the subject 'я' follows the verb for colloquial emphasis and to mark the speaker's hearsay.
segments.p2-7-g88: 'Тешат вас' is third-person plural present ('they amuse/entertain/flatter you'), with 'вас' as the object; here 'тешат' implies flattering or indulging rather than consoling.
segments.p2-7-g89: 'Дур' is a vocative/pejorative address meaning 'fools' (literally 'silly ones'); it stands alone as an interjection directed at the women being spoken to.
segments.p2-7-g90: The clause 'позволяют вам любовников здесь иметь' uses 'позволяют' + dative + infinitive: 'they allow you to have lovers here'. 'Вам' is dative, 'любовников' is the accusative plural object, and 'иметь' is the infinitive governed by 'позволяют'.
segments.p2-7-g91: 'Да ведь это одно баловство' again uses 'да ведь' as an emphatic 'but/after all'; 'это одно баловство' means 'this is merely a bit of fun' — 'одно' stresses the triviality ('mere'). 'Баловство' is a neuter noun in nominative, the predicate of the clause.
segments.p2-7-g92: The short phrase 'один обман' is an appositive: 'one deception' or 'a mere deception' — 'один' again downplays it as 'nothing but'.
segments.p2-7-g93: 'Один смех над вами' literally 'one laughter over you' — here 'смех над вами' means 'derision directed at you'. 'Над' expresses the target of the derision and 'вами' is the instrumental/agent-like form used after 'над'.
segments.p2-7-g94: 'А вы верите.' contrasts the speaker's dismissal with the addressees' credulity: 'а' 'but', 'вы' subject 'you' (plural/formal), and 'верите' present-tense 'believe' (2nd person plural).
segments.p2-7-g95: The opening 'Что он,' is a colloquial exclamatory device: 'What — he...' or 'What, he...?' It cues surprise or skepticism; 'он' is the pronoun 'he'.
segments.p2-7-g96: 'В самом деле' is a fixed phrase meaning 'really' or 'in fact' (literally 'in the very matter'); it's an intensifier questioning the reality of the claim.
segments.p2-7-g97: The tag 'что ли' is colloquial and softens or adds uncertainty to the question — roughly 'is it that (he does)?' The enclitic 'ли' marks the element as interrogative/dubious.
segments.p2-7-g98: 'Любит тебя' is a straightforward present-tense clause with 'любит' (he loves) and object 'тебя' (you). The verb precedes the object in normal order here.
segments.p2-7-g99: The final 'любовник-то?' attaches the particle '-то' to 'любовник' to add pointed emphasis or contrast: 'the lover, then?' It turns the noun into a sharp rhetorical tag in the question.
segments.p2-7-g12: 'Нет,' here is a discourse particle, not a literal denial; it functions as a brief interjection ('No' / 'well') responding to the prior remark.
segments.p2-7-g13: 'видно,' is an impersonal adverb meaning 'apparently' or 'it seems'; it comments on the speaker's judgement and does not agree with any noun.
segments.p2-7-g14: 'много значит привычка' is an inverted, emphatic clause: 'много' (intensifier) + verb 'значит' used in the sense 'to matter' (it is important), with the subject 'привычка' placed after the predicate for force ('habit matters a great deal').
segments.p2-7-g15: 'Черт знает' is a colloquial set phrase/expletive (literally 'the devil knows') used like English 'who knows' or 'God knows'; 'знает' is 3rd person singular present.
segments.p2-7-g16: 'что привычка может из человека сделать' — 'что' is an interrogative/relative 'what' here (object of 'сделать'); 'привычка' is the subject; 'может' is a modal 'can/is able to'; 'из' + genitive 'человека' = 'out of a person'; 'сделать' is the infinitive 'to make' (object is the 'what' clause).
segments.p2-7-g17: Series of particles and the verb: 'Да' is a discourse particle adding emphasis, 'неужели' is a rhetorical 'really?/surely?', 'ж' is an emphatic particle reinforcing 'неужели', then 'ты серьезно думаешь' is the 2nd-person present ('do you seriously think').
segments.p2-7-g18: 'что никогда не состареешься' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'что' ('that'); 'никогда' + 'не' form the standard negation 'never', and 'состареешься' is the future 2nd-person reflexive verb 'to grow old' ('you will grow old'), with '-ся' marking intransitive/change of state.
segments.p2-7-g19: 'вечно хороша будешь' — 'вечно' (always/eternally), 'хороша' is the short-form adjective in the feminine predicate ('be pretty/well'), followed by 'будешь' (future 'you will be'); then 'и что тебя здесь веки вечные держать будут?' is another clause with 'и что' ('and that'), 'тебя' (accusative 'you'), 'здесь' (here), the emphatic temporal phrase 'веки вечные' ('ages eternal' = 'for ages'), and the future passive/active construction 'держать будут' ('they will keep/hold'). Note the inversion and emphatic word order used for force.
segments.p2-7-g20: 'Я не говорю уж про то' — literal 'I am not saying, indeed, about that': 'Я не говорю' is present negation ('I'm not saying'), 'уж' here adds the sense 'even/indeed' (emphatic), and 'про то' ('about that') frames the omitted topic the speaker refuses to discuss.
segments.p2-7-g21: 'что и здесь пакость.' — 'что' introduces the clause ('that'); 'и' functions as 'even/also' ('even here'); 'здесь' is the locative adverb 'here'; 'пакость' is a colloquial noun meaning 'nastiness/nuisance/filth' used predicatively to say the place is unpleasant.
segments.p2-7-g72: "да ведь" is a colloquial, emphatic opening: "да" here functions as a modal/introductory particle (not a literal "yes") and "ведь" strengthens it ("after all/you see"). "это все" is a demonstrative clause: "это" is the demonstrative pronoun "this/that," and "все" (neuter/indefinite) means "everything/that is all" — an elliptical predicate meaning "that is everything."
segments.p2-7-g73: The same emphatic particle pair "да ведь" opens this clause; "это алмаз" is a simple demonstrative copular statement where "это" = "this/that" and "алмаз" is the predicative noun used metaphorically. Word order is neutral: demonstrative + noun-predicate.
segments.p2-7-g74: "девичье" is the neuter singular adjective (agreeing with "сокровище") meaning "maiden's/ girl's." "сокровище" is a neuter noun meaning "treasure"; together they form a noun phrase in apposition to the previous metaphor "алмаз." The adjective is attributive and uses the diminutive/possessive sense.
segments.p2-7-g75: "любовь" is the noun "love." The clitic particle "-то" appended after the noun is an emphatic particle (often written with a hyphen) meaning roughly "-itself/indeed/just," focusing attention on the word it attaches to; the whole exclamation stresses that it is precisely love being spoken about.
segments.p2-7-g76: "Ведь" is an introductory particle meaning "after all/you know." "чтоб" is the colloquial contraction of "чтобы" introducing a purpose/infinitive clause; it is followed by the perfective infinitive "заслужить" ("to deserve/earn") which governs the accusative object "эту любовь" ("this love"). The whole phrase reads as a purpose/conditional infinitival clause: "In order to deserve this love / To deserve this love."
segments.p2-7-g77: "иной" here functions as an indefinite pronoun meaning "someone/another person" (often translated "some"). "готов" is a short-form adjective meaning "ready/willing" and takes an infinitive complement. "душу" is accusative of "душа" ("soul/heart") and is the direct object of the infinitive "положить"; together the predicate is "(some) are ready to lay down (their) soul."
segments.p2-7-g78: "на смерть пойти" is an infinitival phrase meaning literally "to go to death" (an idiom for risking or accepting death). "на" here marks direction/purpose toward the noun in accusative, "смерть" is the noun "death," and "пойти" is the perfective infinitive "to set off/go," used here to indicate undertaking the action of dying.
segments.p2-7-g79: "А" is a contrastive conjunction ("but/and"). "во что" is a prepositional phrase functioning idiomatically with the verb "ценится" to ask "at what (value) / worth what." Here "во" is the preposition variant of "в" used before the pronoun, and "что" is the interrogative "what." "твоя любовь" is the subject ("your love"), and the verb "ценится" (3rd person singular reflexive) means "is valued/valued at." The overall word order places the question-phrase fronted: "And into/at what is your love valued now?"
segments.p2-7-g80: "Ты" is the 2nd-person singular pronoun "you". "вся" is a predicative form (feminine, agreeing with the implied person) meaning "entire/whole" used to emphasize totality. "куплена" is the short-form past passive participle (feminine) of "купить" meaning "bought" (i.e., 'you've been bought' or 'you are bought/paid for'); grammatically it functions as a predicate adjective describing "ты."
segments.p2-7-g81: "вся целиком" repeats and reinforces the totalizing idea: "вся" (whole/all) plus the adverb "целиком" ("entirely/as a whole"). Both together intensify the predicate — stylistic emphasis achieved by duplication of the same basic meaning.
segments.p2-7-g82: "и" links the clauses. "зачем" is a rhetorical interrogative "what for/why." The particle "уж" here adds emphasis or resignation ("really/at that"). "тут" means "here/at this point." "любви" is the genitive of "любовь" required by the verb "добиваться" (which governs genitive when meaning "to strive for/obtain"); "добиваться" in this collocation means "to try to win/obtain (love)" or "to court." The clause asks why one should bother to seek love in the present circumstances.
segments.p2-7-g83: "когда" introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason or circumstance (here: "when/since"). "и" functions as an intensifier here ("even"), so "и без любви" = "even without love." "без" governs the genitive "любви." "все возможно" is an impersonal/predicative construction meaning "everything is possible" ("все" = "everything/all," "возможно" = "possible"). The clause conveys that all can be achieved even in the absence of love.
segments.p2-7-g112: 'Спроси-ка' is an imperative with the colloquial particle '-ка' that softens/urges the command; the direct object 'его' (him) follows the verb as the usual Russian object pronoun.
segments.p2-7-g113: 'Коли' is an older/colloquial conditional 'if/when'. 'Есть' here is existential 'there is / exists' and, together with 'у тебя' (lit. 'at you'), means 'if you have'. Word order 'такой у тебя' = 'such a one at you / that you have.' The colon signals the direct reported question that follows.
segments.p2-7-g114: The clause is a yes/no question formed with the enclitic 'ли' after the verb. 'Женится' is the verb 'to marry' (3rd person singular, future/intent). Word order is verb–subject ('женится он'), and 'на тебе' (with 'на' + pronoun) means 'on/with you' (here colloquial usage = 'marry you').
segments.p2-7-g115: 'Да' here is a colloquial introductory particle ('well/why/and') that introduces the statement. 'Он' is the subject; 'тебе' is dative 'at you/to you'; 'в глаза' is a literal 'into the eyes' idiom meaning 'in your face'; 'расхохочется' is perfective, 3rd person singular, reflexive: 'will burst out laughing'.
segments.p2-7-g116: 'Если только не' functions as 'unless.' Both verbs are future, 3rd person singular, with 'не' forming the negative possibility: 'наплюет' (literally 'will spit' — here 'spit on you') or 'прибьет' (here colloquial/hyperbolic 'will beat/kill/knock you down'). 'Иль' is an archaic/poetic 'or.'
segments.p2-7-g117: 'А' is a contrastive conjunction 'and/but'; 'ему' is dative 'to him' used here as a topicalizer meaning 'as for him'; 'самому' is emphatic ('himself' / 'to himself') stressing personal perspective.
segments.p2-7-g118: Short particle 'может' = 'may/maybe' (colloquial short for 'может быть'), indicating possibility; it stands alone and modifies the following evaluative clause.
segments.p2-7-g119: 'Всей-то цены' literally 'the whole (emphatic) value' — 'то' is an emphatic particle attached to 'всей'. The dash links this topic to its apposition 'два сломанных гроша' ('two broken groschen'), where 'сломанных' is an adjective/participle agreeing with 'гроша' in case/number (genitive after the numeral). Overall: 'his entire worth may be only two broken coins.'
segments.p2-7-g120: 'И' = 'and' linking to previous thought; 'за что' is the idiom 'for what / what for' introducing a rhetorical question about motive.
segments.p2-7-g121: 'Подумаешь' is a parenthetical colloquial expression meaning 'one might think' or 'you'd think' (often ironic or dismissive); here it interrupts the question for ironic effect.
segments.p2-7-g122: Standard S–(adjunct)–O–V order: 'ты' (you) subject, 'здесь' (here) adverbial, 'жизнь свою' (your life — 'свою' reflexive possessive agreeing with subject) is the direct object, and 'погубила' is past tense, feminine singular perfective 'ruined/ruin(ed)'. The whole is a rhetorical question: 'did you ruin your life here?'.
segments.p2-7-g123: 'Что' is an exclamatory/interrogative particle here (expressing incredulity). 'Тебя' is the object 'you'; 'кофеем' is instrumental, indicating what you are given (literally 'with coffee' or 'coffee as a drink'); 'поят' is 3rd person plural present 'they give (you) drink'; 'да' connects the second verb; 'кормят' is 3rd person plural 'they feed'; 'сытно' is an adverb meaning 'satisfyingly/heartily.' Together it expresses scornful surprise that you are merely being given coffee and well fed.
segments.p2-7-g265: Чахотка (an archaic name for consumption/tuberculosis) is the grammatical subject in the nominative; такая + болезнь is an appositive-like noun phrase ('such a disease') following it with no copula—this word order states a categorical identification. Такая is a demonstrative/qualifying adjective modifying болезнь, emphasizing the sort or nature of the disease.
segments.p2-7-g266: Это here functions as a predicative/identifying pronoun ('it/this is'), followed by the negator не and the noun горячка in the nominative; Russian omits the present copula, so the structure literally reads 'it not fever' = 'it is not a fever.'
segments.p2-7-g267: Тут is an adverbial ('here' or 'at this point') placing the scene; до + genitive (до последней минуты) forms a temporal phrase 'until the last minute.' Человек is the subject and the two verbs надеется и говорит are coordinated 3rd-person singular present verbs ('hopes and says'), indicating ongoing actions up to that time.
segments.p2-7-g268: Что introduces a subordinate content clause ('that ...') and здоров is the short-form adjective used predicatively in the nominative masculine singular, with the copula omitted (i.e. 'that he is healthy').
segments.p2-7-g269: Сам себя is an emphatic combination: сам ('himself' / emphatic) before the accusative себя ('himself') stresses that the subject acts on himself. Тешит is 3rd-person singular present ('consoles'/'amuses'); the clause has no auxiliary and means 'he comforts/soothes himself.'
segments.p2-7-g270: А introduces a contrast ('and/but'); хозяйке is dative ('for the landlady' or 'to the landlady') and carries the focus marker -то (postpositive particle) which emphasizes or contrasts that person. И выгодно is an impersonal predicate: и adds emphasis ('even/also'), and выгодно (neuter/adverbial form) means 'it is profitable' — together meaning 'and for the landlady it's even profitable.'
segments.p2-7-g136: Тут (here/at this point) + ведь (particle meaning 'you know/after all') frame the clause with a colloquial, emphatic stance. Это всё is the topicalized nominal phrase ('all this') and летит is a 3rd-person singular present (used like a general/future inevitability here) meaning 'flies/is carried off'. На почтовых is an elliptical prepositional phrase (short for 'на почтовых каретах') meaning 'on/by the mail coaches' and indicates means of transport.
segments.p2-7-g137: Тебя is the fronted accusative object ('you'), placed first for emphasis; the grammatical subject ('they' — unspecified people) is omitted as usual in Russian. И here functions as an emphatic/affirmative particle ('even/also'), and вытолкают is the 3rd-person plural future perfective ('will push out, will expel').
segments.p2-7-g138: Да и is a colloquial linking phrase here meaning 'and besides' or 'moreover'; не просто ('not simply') negates the manner; вытолкают is 3rd-person-plural future perfective ('they will push out'), so the whole clause says they won't merely eject you but will do more.
segments.p2-7-g139: А introduces contrast ('but/and'), задолго сначала is a stacked adverbial ('long beforehand' / 'well in advance') and придираться (infinitive imperfective) means 'to pick at / find fault with'. Начнут is 3rd-person-plural future of 'начать' ('will begin'), so 'they will begin to pick at you long before (they push you out)'.
segments.p2-7-g140: Попрекать (infinitive) 'to reproach/taunt' followed by начнут ('will begin') continues the series of verbs introduced by 'начнут' — a repeated pattern listing actions they will start doing; word order places the infinitive before the finite verb for a clipped, spoken rhythm.
segments.p2-7-g141: Ругать (infinitive) 'to scold/insult' again pairs with the repeated начнут; the repetition emphasizes a sequence of hostile actions. The infinitive + начнут construction signals actions that will commence habitually or repeatedly.
segments.p2-7-g142: Как будто introduces a hypothetical/comparative clause ('as if/as though'). Не before ты negates the implied past-giving ('as if you had not...'); ты is the stressed subject. Ей is the dative 'to her', здоровье своё is a possessive phrase 'your health' (свое reflexive possessive agreeing with ты), and отдала is past tense perfective 'gave over/surrendered'. Together: 'as if you hadn't given her your health.'
segments.p2-7-g143: Молодость и душу ('youth and soul') are direct objects; даром here means 'in vain/for nothing' rather than merely 'for free', and для неё ('for her') gives the beneficiary. Загубила is past-feminine perfective 'ruined/destroyed', referring back to the implied subject ты. The clause accuses her of wasting her youth and soul on the woman.
segments.p2-7-g144: А как будто ('but as if') continues the sarcastic counterfactuals. Ты-то uses the contrastive particle -то on 'ты' to single the addressee out ('you, of all people'); ее is 'her' (object), и is a linking/emphatic particle here ('even/and'), and разорила is past-feminine 'ruined' (often with the sense 'bankrupted' or 'devastated'). The construction stresses the absurd reversal of blame.
segments.p2-7-g145: По миру is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'into the world/through society' (по + мир), and пустила is past-feminine of пустить ('sent/released/let go'); together 'she set her adrift in the world' or 'sent her out into the world'. This is a compact accusatory phrase continuing the list of alleged injuries.
segments.p2-7-g146: Обокрала is a single-word perfective past predicate ('robbed' or 'deprived'), a blunt accusation placed as the final, emphatic verb. The past-feminine form matches the implied subject ты and completes the litany of charges.
segments.p2-7-g147: И functions as a simple connective ('and/also') starting the sentence. Не + жди is a negative imperative (2nd-person singular): 'don't expect' or 'don't wait for.' Поддержки is a genitive singular noun used as the object of ждать (ждать чего), 'support/assistance.' The colon introduces the explanation or consequences that follow.
segments.p2-7-g148: Другие подруги is the noun phrase 'other (female) friends'; the postpositive particle то after подруги (подруги-то) is emphatic/contrastive, marking attention to that group. Твои is the possessive 'your'. Тоже is an adverb 'also/too'. На тебя is the prepositional-object phrase ('against you'/'upon you'). Нападут is future tense, 3rd-person plural of нападать/напасть, used figuratively here as 'will attack/turn on.'
segments.p2-7-g149: Чтоб here introduces a purpose clause ('so that/in order that'). Ей is the dative pronoun 'to her'. Подслужиться is a reflexive infinitive meaning 'to ingratiate oneself' or 'to curry favor'; together the clause expresses the motive of the attack: they will attack in order to gain favor with her.
segments.p2-7-g150: Потому что is the causal conjunction 'because.' Здесь is a locative/adverb ('here' in the situational sense). Все is the indefinite pronoun 'everyone' (subject carried forward). В рабстве is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in slavery' or 'in servitude' (literal or figurative), describing the condition of 'everyone.'
segments.p2-7-g151: Совесть и жалость are the coordinated nouns 'conscience and pity.' Давнo is an adverb modifying the verb, meaning 'long ago' or 'for a long time.' Потеряли is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of потерять ('they lost'), agreeing with все from the previous clause: 'everyone has long since lost conscience and pity.'
segments.p2-7-g124: This whole short clause is a colloquial, emphatic question. Да here functions as an exclamatory/emphatic particle (roughly 'well'/'but'), ведь adds 'after all' or 'you know' to strengthen the speaker's assumption, для чего is the question 'for what purpose/why', кормят is 3rd-person plural present 'they feed' with no explicit object (implying 'you' or 'people'), and the enclitic-то on the end is a colloquial emphatic particle highlighting contrast or impatience. Word order is typical: particles + question phrase + verb, and the sentence is rhetorical.
segments.p2-7-g125: У другой бы is an idiomatic conditional opening: у + genitive (другой) means 'in the case of another person' or 'another (one)', and the particle бы marks the counterfactual/conditional mood ('would'). The phrase sets up a hypothetical contrast.
segments.p2-7-g126: Честной is an appositive adjective (feminine singular) qualifying 'другой' and is set off by commas for emphasis: 'an honest one.' Here it functions as a characterizing descriptor rather than as part of a predicate.
segments.p2-7-g127: В горло is 'into the throat' with в + accusative showing direction; такой кусок is the subject ('such a piece' or 'such a morsel'); не пошел is the negated past form of пойти used here idiomatically — 'would not go (down)' — so the whole clause means 'such a morsel would not have gone down into her throat.'
segments.p2-7-g128: Потому что introduces the reason clause ('because'), and знает is 3rd-person singular present 'she knows' (the subject is the same 'another' implied earlier). Together they mean 'because she knows.'
segments.p2-7-g129: Для чего is the question phrase 'for what purpose/why' and кормят is again 3rd-person plural 'they feed' (here passive in sense: 'she is fed' or 'they feed her'); the clause states the content of what she knows: 'what for they feed (her).'
segments.p2-7-g130: Ты is the 2nd-person singular subject, здесь is an adverb 'here' modifying the predicate, and должна is a predicative adjective meaning 'obliged/indebted' (note feminine form — addressing a woman or using feminine predicate). The clause states a present condition: 'You are obliged here.'
segments.p2-7-g131: This is a colloquial, repetitive piling-on for emphasis: ну и все is a dismissive 'well, that's that' or intensifier; будешь is the 2nd-person future of быть ('you will be'); должна repeats the predicate 'obliged'; the word order alternates (будешь должна / должна будешь) as a stylistic echo. До конца концов is an emphatic idiom meaning 'to the very end' and the repetition stresses inevitability.
segments.p2-7-g132: До тех самых пор is a frozen temporal phrase meaning 'until those very times' or more naturally 'until that very moment'; до governs the genitive тех (those) and самих (the very) modifies пор (times/moments).
segments.p2-7-g133: Что introduces the subordinate clause ('that'/'when'), тобой is the instrumental pronoun 'by you' used with брезгать (the verb takes an object in the instrumental to mean 'to disdain someone'), гости is the subject 'the guests', брезгать is the infinitive 'to disdain' (used here as the predicate verb), and начнут is 3rd-person plural future 'they will begin'. The clause thus means 'until the guests begin to disdain you.'
segments.p2-7-g134: А is a connective particle linking or contrasting with the previous statement (here: 'and/then'). Это is the demonstrative subject ('this' — the situation just described). Скоро is a temporal adverb modifying the verb. Придет is the simple future, 3rd person singular of прийти (perfective), used to state an inevitable event; the comma signals the sentence will continue with a related admonition.
segments.p2-7-g135: Не + imperative (надейся) is a negative imperative: 'don't hope/rely'. Надейся is the 2nd-person singular imperative of надеяться. На governs the accusative noun молодость, meaning 'rely on/expect from youth.' The whole clause is an admonition linked to the previous clause by the comma.
segments.p2-7-g152: Исподлились is a past‑tense, plural, reflexive/short verbal form (perfective aspect) meaning 'have acted basely' or 'have become base'; the plural subject is implied ('they' or 'people'), and the verb begins the clause that the following comparatives amplify.
segments.p2-7-g153: и уж гаже is a short comparative phrase: 'и' here functions as an additive/emphatic particle ('and / even'), 'уж' is a strengthening particle meaning 'indeed' or 'all the more', and 'гаже' is the comparative form 'worse' — the phrase intensifies the change just stated and links to further comparatives.
segments.p2-7-g154: подлее is the comparative form of подлый ('vile' / 'base'), used predicatively/adverbially in a series of comparatives; it continues the catalogue of ways they have become worse and stands in parallel with the other comparatives by agreement in function.
segments.p2-7-g155: обиднее этих ругательств и на земле не бывает is an impersonal construction stating a universal negative: 'there is not (бывает) on earth (на земле) anything more offensive (обиднее) than these insults (этих ругательств)'. 'Обиднее' is a comparative, 'этих ругательств' is genitive plural used with the comparative to mark what it is compared to, and 'не бывает' is the negative existential 'does not occur / there is not'.
segments.p2-7-g156: И все-то ты здесь положишь is emphatic and colloquial: 'все-то' (все + particle то) stresses 'everything', placed before the subject 'ты' in an inverted word order; 'здесь' is a place adverb modifying the verb, and 'положишь' is second‑person singular future indicative of положить ('to lay down' / here idiomatically 'to lose / give up').
segments.p2-7-g157: все, is a repetition of 'everything' for emphasis; punctuated as a resumptive element that introduces the following enumerated items to be 'laid down' or lost.
segments.p2-7-g158: без завета is a prepositional phrase where без governs the genitive; it means 'without a testament/last will' or more generally 'without any bequest/legacy', qualifying how everything will be lost.
segments.p2-7-g159: - и здоровье, begins the enumerated list of things lost: the coordinating и ('and') links items, and здоровье is the direct object (accusative) of положишь — 'your health'.
segments.p2-7-g160: и молодость, continues the coordinated list: 'and youth' (молодость acting as another object in the same series).
segments.p2-7-g161: и красоту, another item in the enumeration: 'and beauty' in the accusative, parallel to the previous objects.
segments.p2-7-g162: и надежды, 'and hopes' — here надежды is plural and functions as a direct object; formally identical to nominative plural but context makes it an object in the list of things to be lost.
segments.p2-7-g163: и в двадцать два года будешь смотреть как тридцатипятилетняя is a temporal/resultative clause: 'в двадцать два года' is a time/age expression ('at twenty‑two years'), 'будешь смотреть' is future imperfective 2nd person singular ('you will look / will appear'), 'как' introduces the comparison 'like', and тридцатипятилетняя is a substantive/feminine adjective 'a thirty‑five‑year‑old' agreeing implicitly with ты.
segments.p2-7-g164: и хорошо еще, is an idiomatic tag meaning 'and that's still good/fortunate' — 'и' links back to the clause, 'хорошо' expresses that this would be a comparatively good outcome, and 'еще' adds the sense 'even / still'.
segments.p2-7-g165: коль не больная, is a conditional subordinate phrase: 'коль' is an archaic/colloquial conditional particle meaning 'if', 'не' negates, and 'больная' is a predicate adjective meaning 'ill' (feminine) referring back to the addressed 'ты' — 'if (you are) not ill.'
segments.p2-7-g166: моли бога за это is an imperative clause: 'моли' is the 2nd person singular imperative of молить ('beseech / pray'), 'бога' is the direct object (accusative) 'God', and 'за это' ('for this') marks the object/cause of the prayer — 'pray to God for that.'
segments.p2-7-g167: Ведь is a discourse particle meaning 'after all' or 'you know,' used to remind or emphasize. Ты is the 2nd-person singular subject. Теперь is the adverb 'now.' Небось is a colloquial particle meaning 'probably' or 'I suppose.' Думаешь is present-tense, 2nd-person singular of думать, 'you think,' marking the speaker's guess about the addressee's belief.
segments.p2-7-g168: Что introduces an indirect statement ('that'). Тебе is the dative pronoun used idiomatically here with нет (тебе нет) to indicate possession/state ('for you' / 'you have'). И before работы is an emphatic particle here (≈ 'even' or 'really'), strengthening the negation. Работы is the genitive of работа used with нет to mean 'no work' or 'no job.' Нет is the existential negator 'there is not' (here: 'you have none').
segments.p2-7-g169: Гульба is a colloquial noun meaning 'revelry' or 'carousing.' Standing alone with an exclamation mark it functions as an interjection: the speaker is summing up the addressee's belief as mere partying rather than real work.
segments.p2-7-g170: The whole sentence is an emphatic existential negation. "Да" here functions as an interjection/particle (roughly "indeed" or reinforcing what was just said). "Тяжеле и каторжнее" are two comparative adjectives joined by "и" used as an intensifier ("even more heavy and even more like penal labour"). "Работы" is in the genitive because the verb "нет" takes a genitive object ("there is no ..."). "На свете" is a locative expression meaning "in the world." The final clause "и никогда не бывало" repeats the negation in the past: "не бывало" is the impersonal past meaning "has never occurred/been."
segments.p2-7-g171: Одно сердце is a noun phrase with одно as an emphatic numeral/adjective meaning "one" (here: 'one single') modifying сердце ('heart' = feelings). The phrase is topicalized at the start (subject).
segments.p2-7-g172: кажется is an impersonal present used to signal the narrator's estimation—'it seems' or 'apparently.' It doesn't take a subject here and simply hedges the statement.
segments.p2-7-g173: все бы слезами изошло combines все (here=entirely) with the conditional particle бы + past-form изошло (perfective/preterite form of изойти used like 'would have flowed/issued out') to express a hypothetical result: 'would have completely flowed out in tears.' слезами is an instrumental plural denoting the means/manner ('with tears').
segments.p2-7-g174: И is a connective 'and' introducing the clause; ни слова is an idiomatic negative phrase meaning 'not a word' (ни intensifies the negation). посмеешь is future/conditional-like 2nd person singular of посметь ('to dare') and is followed by the infinitive сказать as its complement—'you will not dare to say.'
segments.p2-7-g175: ни полслова repeats the negation for emphasis: ни ('not even') + полслова literally 'half a word' used idiomatically 'not even half a word.' The phrase is elliptical (no verb here) and reinforces the previous clause.
segments.p2-7-g176: когда introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; тебя is the accusative object placed before the verb for natural word order/emphasis; погонят is future 3rd-person plural of погнать (perfective) meaning 'they will drive/expel'; отсюда is the adverb 'from here.'
segments.p2-7-g177: пойдешь is 2nd-person singular future of пойти ('you will go') used here intransitively; как introduces a manner comparison 'as/like'; виноватая is a predicate adjective (feminine form) functioning as the complement to как: 'you'll go like someone guilty.' The subject ты is omitted but understood.
segments.p2-7-g178: Перейдешь is 2nd-person singular future of перейти here meaning 'you will change/move (to another place)'; the subject ты follows the verb (inversion for style). в другое место is the destination phrase with в + accusative.
segments.p2-7-g179: потом is a temporal adverb 'then/afterwards'; the verb is omitted here (elliptical) and understood to be repeated from the previous clause; в третье means 'into a third [place]' with третий as ordinal adjective.
segments.p2-7-g180: потом еще куда-нибудь uses потом 'then', ещё 'further/else', куда-нибудь (куда + -нибудь) as an indefinite 'somewhere/anywhere'—together 'then off somewhere else.' и links to the final clause; доберешься is the future reflexive verb добраться in 2nd-person singular 'you'll reach/manage to get to'; наконец 'finally' modifies the action; до + Сенной marks the goal ('to Sennaya', the haymarket name).
segments.p2-7-g181: А is a contrasting/continuative conjunction 'and'/'but' starting the sentence; там 'there' sets location; уж is an intensifier meaning 'already' or 'indeed'; походя is an adverbial form meaning roughly 'in passing' or 'while passing' (here 'casually/in passing'); бить is the infinitive 'to beat/strike' and начнут is 3rd-person plural future of начать 'they will begin'—the finite verb начнут governs the infinitive bить.
segments.p2-7-g182: это is the demonstrative pronoun 'this/that' introducing a nominal predicate; любезность is a feminine noun 'courtesy/politeness'; тамошняя is an attributive adjective meaning 'of that place' or 'local' and agrees with любезность. The phrase states a generalizing identification: 'this is the local courtesy.'
segments.p2-7-g183: там 'there' again marks place; гость is the noun 'guest' (subject in this clause); и here functions as an emphatic 'even' (not merely additive) before the infinitive приласкать ('to caress'), which is left hanging to be completed by the following negative construction. The clause is intentionally elliptical/staccato.
segments.p2-7-g184: не прибив is an adverbial perfective gerund (деепричастие) from прибить with negative не: 'not having [nailed/knocked/beaten].' It functions adverbially to qualify the main verb in the next segment—'without having beaten.'
segments.p2-7-g185: не умеет is a simple present (3rd sing.) of уметь with negation: 'does not know how / cannot.' It takes the preceding infinitive приласкать as its object (implied from the earlier clause), so the whole means 'doesn't know how to caress (someone) without having beaten them first.'
segments.p2-7-g186: Ты is the informal 2nd-person singular subject. Не is the negation particle that directly negates the verb. Веришь is the present indicative, 2nd-person singular of верить (‘to believe’); the clause is a negative main clause and the trailing comma introduces the subordinate clause that completes the thought.
segments.p2-7-g187: Что here is a complementizer introducing a subordinate clause (‘that…’). Там is a locative adverb ‘there’. Так is an intensifier ‘so/that way’. Противно is a short-form/neuter predicative (from противный) meaning ‘unpleasant/disgusting’ and serves as the clause predicate; the whole clause supplies what the speaker is said not to believe.
segments.p2-7-g188: Ступай is an informal imperative (from ступать) meaning ‘go on’ or ‘set out’; here it functions as a colloquial command/encouragement and is followed by a comma to soften the sequence of imperatives.
segments.p2-7-g189: Посмотри is a perfective imperative of посмотреть ‘take a look’ (an invitation to try/see). Когда-нибудь is the fused adverbial phrase meaning ‘sometime/ever’ (когда – temporal interrogative/relative, and нибудь is the indefinite/ever particle), giving an indefinite time for the action.
segments.p2-7-g190: Может is a colloquial modal particle (abbreviation of может быть) meaning ‘maybe’ or ‘perhaps’; it modifies the following clause and signals possibility rather than ability.
segments.p2-7-g191: Своими глазами is an instrumental-phrase ‘with your own eyes’ (свои used reflexively/possessively to stress ownership). Увидишь is the perfective future, 2nd-person singular of увидеть (‘you will see’); together this predicts a possible direct perception by the addressee.
segments.p2-7-g192: Я is the 1st-person subject. Вон раз is a colloquial temporal expression meaning ‘once/one time’ (вон is a demonstrative/colloquial marker + раз ‘time/once’). Видел is the past tense (masculine) of видеть ‘saw’ (narrator recounting a past event). Там on Новый год is a time/place phrase: там ‘there’ and на Новый год indicates the occasion/time (‘on New Year's’). Одну (not listed among the words here) is the feminine accusative object ‘one [woman]’ and completes the verb's object.
segments.p2-7-g193: У + genitive (дверей) indicates location near or at something: ‘by the door(s)’. Дверей is genitive plural here but idiomatically means ‘at the door/doorway’. The short sentence-final phrase gives the precise location of what the narrator saw.
segments.p2-7-g194: Ее is the accusative object pronoun ‘her’ (the person pushed). Вытолкали is past plural perfective ‘pushed out’ (the main verb). В насмешку is an adverbial phrase ‘in mockery/to make fun of her’. Свои же literally ‘their own, indeed/the very ones’ (свои = their own people; же is an emphatic particle stressing that it was their own people). Проморозить (infinitive) here expresses the result/intention of leaving her outside (‘to let/get frostbitten’), маленько softens it ‘a little’, and за то begins the subordinate clause ‘for the fact that…’ and links to the explanation that follows.
segments.p2-7-g195: Что is the complementizer ‘that’ introducing the reason clause. Уж is an intensifying particle here (roughly ‘really/so’), очень ‘very’, and ревела is the imperfective past tense ‘was crying’ (she cried/was sobbing); together they state the cause: she was crying so much.
segments.p2-7-g196: А is a coordinating conjunction meaning ‘and’ / ‘but’ that shifts to the next action. Дверь is the direct object ‘the door’. За ней is a prepositional phrase ‘after her/behind her’ indicating the door was closed in her wake. Притворили is past plural of притворить meaning here ‘to half-close/close (a door slightly)’ (not ‘to pretend’), describing the act of shutting the door behind her.
segments.p2-7-g211: Ты is the familiar 2nd-person singular subject 'you'. Не is the negation placed before the verb. Веришь is 2nd-person singular present indicative of верить meaning 'to believe' — here it forms the main clause 'you do not believe'. The phrase, with question intonation, functions as a negative question (expecting confirmation).
segments.p2-7-g212: Что introduces a subordinate declarative clause 'that ...'. И here means 'also/too' (you, too). Ты is the repeated subject in the subordinate clause. Такая же is a two-word descriptive phrase: такая 'such/like that' + же the emphatic particle giving 'that very/same'. Будешь is 2nd-person singular future of быть, here 'you will be' or 'you'll become/turn out to be'. Together: 'that you too will be the same.'
segments.p2-7-g213: И is a connective 'and' (here linking to the previous thought). Я is the 1st-person subject. Бы is the modal/conditional particle that softens хотел into a conditional 'would like'. Не before хотел negates it. Хотел is past-tense masculine of хотеть used with бы to mean 'would like' or 'would want'. Верить is the infinitive 'to believe' — the object of хотел: 'I would not like to believe (it).' The object is implicit (elliptical).
segments.p2-7-g214: А introduces a contrast 'but'. Почем is colloquial for 'отчего/почему' meaning 'how come' or 'why/how do you know' here. Ты is the 2nd-person subject and знаешь is present 2nd-person of знать 'you know'. The whole clause means 'but how do you know?' — a challenge to the addressee's certainty.
segments.p2-7-g215: Может быть is the fixed two-word modal 'perhaps/maybe' formed from может (possibility) + быть ('to be'). It signals uncertainty about the following temporal estimate.
segments.p2-7-g216: Лет here is the genitive plural form used after numerals to count years; it heads the temporal phrase meaning 'some ten years' (approximate).
segments.p2-7-g217: Назад placed after the numeral gives the 'ago' sense ('eight years ago'). The sequence 'лет десять, восемь назад' indicates an approximate time span (ten, eight years ago).
segments.p2-7-g218: Эта is the feminine demonstrative 'this/that'. Же is an emphatic particle reinforcing identity ('this very'). Самая as a superlative/demonstrative here means 'the very one' — together 'this very same [woman]'.
segments.p2-7-g219: С + noun in instrumental (рыбой) means 'with (a) fish' — indicating she had the salted fish with her. Соленой is the feminine instrumental adjective 'salted' agreeing with рыбой. The enclitic -то after соленой (соленой-то) is colloquial emphasis ('that very salted fish' / drawing attention to the fish).
segments.p2-7-g220: Приехала is the feminine past tense of приехать 'arrived (by vehicle / came)'. Сюда 'to here' marks direction. Откуда-нибудь is a compound of откуда 'from where' + -нибудь (indefinite particle) meaning 'from somewhere'. Свеженькая is a diminutive/adjective 'fresh' with affectionate nuance ('fresh, lively, newly arrived'). Word order places the verb before its adverbial complements.
segments.p2-7-g221: Как introduces a simile 'like/as'. Херувимчик is a diminutive 'little cherub' (affectionate image); the diminutive -чик makes the simile more endearing.
segments.p2-7-g222: Невинная is a feminine adjective meaning 'innocent/naïve' here describing moral/naïve innocence rather than legal innocence. It continues the catalogue of virtues assigned to her on arrival.
segments.p2-7-g223: Чистенькая is a diminutive/adjective meaning 'clean/neat' with a tender or approving connotation ('pretty clean/tidy'). The diminutive ending softens the description.
segments.p2-7-g224: Зла here is the genitive of зло and functions idiomatically in 'зла не знала' — 'knew no evil' or 'knew nothing of malice'. Не negates знала, past tense of знать; the construction expresses she was free from malice or harm.
segments.p2-7-g225: На каждом слове is a prepositional phrase 'at/on every word/remark' (на + prepositional case), idiomatically 'at every remark'. Краснела is feminine past 'she blushed' — the resultative reaction repeated for each remark.
segments.p2-7-g226: "Может быть" is a fixed modal phrase expressing possibility ("perhaps"/"maybe"); grammatically it functions as an adverbial/modal qualifier for the whole clause that follows.
segments.p2-7-g227: "Такая же" is a demonstrative adjective phrase meaning "such/that sort (of person) — the same as (you)." The particle "же" is emphatic, reinforcing the comparison to the addressee.
segments.p2-7-g228: "Как ты" is the comparative clause "as you" ("like you"); "ты" is the familiar singular subject being compared to.
segments.p2-7-g229: "Была" is the past-tense, feminine singular of "быть" ("was"), predicating the preceding description of her character.
segments.p2-7-g230: "Гордая" is a nominative feminine adjective meaning "proud" (describing the subject); it continues the series of character traits.
segments.p2-7-g231: "Обидчивая" is another nominative feminine adjective meaning "touchy/resentful," continuing the list of qualities.
segments.p2-7-g232: "На других не похожая" literally "not like others" — "на" plus the plural form "других" marks the comparison target, and "не похожая" is the participial/adjectival phrase "not resembling." Together they mean "unlike other people."
segments.p2-7-g233: "Королевной смотрела" is an idiomatic expression meaning "she looked/appeared like a princess" — here "королевной" is a nominal (rendering "princess") used as the complement to "смотрела" (appeared as). "И сама знала" adds "and herself knew" ("сама" emphasizes that she personally knew it).
segments.p2-7-g234: "Что целое счастье того ожидает" introduces a content clause with "что" ("that"). "Целое счастье" means "complete/whole happiness." "Того" is a demonstrative/genitive pronoun referring forward to the person described by the following relative clause ("that one/the person"). "Ожидает" is present-tense "awaits/is in store for."
segments.p2-7-g235: Two parallel hypothetical relative clauses introduced by the subjunctive particle "бы": "кто бы её полюбил" — "whoever might love her" ("кто бы...полюбил": subjunctive/hypothetical), and "кого бы она полюбила" — "whom she might love." Both use past-tense forms with "бы" to express hypothetical/would-be cases of loving.
segments.p2-7-g236: Видишь is the 2nd-person singular present (or present-tense use) of видеть used here as a conversational particle meaning 'do you see' / 'you see' (often equivalent to 'you understand' or 'aren't you aware'). It's an informal, direct address to the listener and introduces the question that follows.
segments.p2-7-g237: чем is the interrogative pronoun in the instrumental case used with the reflexive verb кончиться to ask 'with what / into what' something ended; кончилось is the past tense, neuter singular, of the reflexive verb кончиться (perfective), meaning 'ended' or 'came to an end' — neuter because the subject is an impersonal/situation 'it'. Together they form the question 'what did it end in / how did it end?'.
segments.p2-7-g197: В девять-то часов утра — a time adverbial placed at the front: в (preposition) + девять часов утра (ordinal time expression). The enclitic particle -то attached to девять (девять-то) is an emphatic particle that stresses the exact hour. Она уж была — past tense, feminine singular of быть (была) with the particle уж meaning 'already'; совсем пьяная is a predicative adjective phrase (совсем = intensifier + пьяная = feminine nominative), all agreeing with the subject она.
segments.p2-7-g198: растрепанная — past participle / adjective (feminine nominative) meaning 'tousled/unkempt', used here as one item in a loose adjectival list describing она (no copula needed because it continues the predicate).
segments.p2-7-g199: полунагая — compound adjective (полу- prefix + нагaя) in the feminine nominative, colloquial 'half-naked'; it continues the series of predicative adjectives modifying она.
segments.p2-7-g200: вся избитая — вся here functions as an intensifying/determinative adjective meaning 'entirely/all' agreeing in gender with the feminine subject, and избитая is the past passive participle (feminine nominative) 'beaten' used predicatively; together they complete the adjectival list describing her condition.
segments.p2-7-g201: Сама набелена — сама is an emphatic/agentive pronoun here (literally 'herself' / 'by herself') and набелена is a short/form past participle (or resultative adjective) meaning 'whitewashed/powdered'. Together they imply that she herself was powdered or that her face was powdered (result state).
segments.p2-7-g202: а глаза в черняках — а is a contrastive conjunction 'but/and', глаза is the nominative plural 'eyes', and в + черняках is a locative-expression meaning 'in blackness/black smears' (here: rimmed or smeared with kohl). The whole phrase contrasts with the powdered face just mentioned.
segments.p2-7-g203: из носа и из зубов кровь течет — two sources introduced by из ('from') linked with и ('and'); кровь is the grammatical subject and течет is present-tense (indicative) 'flows/runs', a vivid descriptive present used to report a continuing physical fact.
segments.p2-7-g204: извозчик какой-то только что починил — извозчик какой‑то is an indefinite subject 'some cabman' (какой‑то = 'some/one of those'), только что is a compound adverb meaning 'just now', and починил is past tense masculine singular of починить 'to fix/repair' used transitively; the direct object is omitted and must be recovered from context (i.e. 'had just fixed/handled her' or 'had just done it').
segments.p2-7-g205: Села она на каменной лесенке — Села is the past feminine singular of сесть 'sat down' (verb before subject for emphasis), она is the subject; на каменной лесенке is a locative prepositional phrase ('on the small stone staircase/steps'), with каменной agreeing in gender/case/number with лесенке.
segments.p2-7-g206: в руках у ней какая-то соленая рыба была — this is a possession/existence construction: в руках у ней ... была literally 'in her hands there was some salted fish'. У + pronoun (у ней) marks possession ('she had'), какая‑то is an indefinite modifier 'some kind of', соленая рыба is the noun phrase, and была is the past copula indicating the fish's presence.
segments.p2-7-g207: она ревела — simple past description: она (she) + ревела, imperfective past 'was bawling/was crying loudly', reporting her noisy lament.
segments.p2-7-g208: что-то причитала про свою "учась" — что‑то is an indefinite pronoun 'something' (что + -то), причитала is a past imperfective/iterative of причитать 'to bewail/complain aloud' (she was uttering laments), про свою marks the topic of complaints 'about her own ...', and учась is quoted as the exact word/sound she used (a gerund-like form or jargon), so the phrase means she was lamenting something about her 'учась' (the word she kept saying).
segments.p2-7-g209: а рыбой колотила по лестничным ступеням — а marks contrast/continuation 'and/but', рыбой is the instrumental singular 'with a fish' (the instrument she used), колотила is the past feminine imperfective 'was beating/was striking' (repetitive/action in progress in the past), and по лестничным ступеням indicates the target 'against/on the stair steps' (по + dative plural meaning 'along/against the steps').
segments.p2-7-g210: "А" opens the sentence as a simple conjunction meaning roughly "and/then." "У крыльца" is a spatial phrase with "у" + genitive meaning "by/at the porch." "Столпились" is past plural of the perfective/reflexive verb "столпиться" meaning "crowded together/thronged." The coordinated noun phrase "извозчики да пьяные солдаты" uses the archaic particle "да" as "and" to link the two groups. "И дразнили её" is a continuing past action (they taunted/jeered at her); "ее" is the accusative object "her."
segments.p2-7-g332: This is a terse, colloquial interrogative. Чего here is an exclamatory/interrogative particle (elliptical for Что ты имеешь в виду?), used to challenge or ask for clarification. Ладно is quoted from prior speech and functions as the thing being questioned — literally 'all right' or 'all right then'.
segments.p2-7-g333: Ишь is a colloquial interjection meaning 'look' or 'would you just see'; it expresses surprise or mild contempt. На боку is a prepositional phrase marking physical position ('on (his) side'), and лежит is third-person singular present indicative of лежать, describing the resulting state 'lies / is lying'.
segments.p2-7-g334: This is an informal alternative question. Человек here means 'person / man' (asking if he counts as a human being), and тоже means 'also / too'. Был is the past-tense copula 'was'. Али ... нет is an older or dialectal pairing equivalent to 'or ... not' (modern Russian would use ли or 'или нет'). The phrase asks whether he was a man as well or not.
segments.p2-7-g335: Ну is a discourse particle meaning 'well' or 'now' (softening or moving the conversation on). Да here functions as a linking/confirming particle inside the fixed colloquial phrase да ладно, and ладно is the core word meaning 'all right' / 'never mind'. Together they form the idiom 'Well, all right' or 'Never mind.'
segments.p2-7-g336: Засыпай is the second-person singular imperative of засыпать meaning 'fall asleep' or more naturally here 'go to sleep' — a direct command or encouragement addressed to the listener. The form is colloquial and familiar.
segments.p2-7-g24: "А впрочем" is a two-word discourse particle: "а" is adversative/contrastive and "впрочем" = "however/anyway"; together they mean "and yet" / "anyway" and introduce a remark. The comma marks a pause before the main clause.
segments.p2-7-g25: Literal order: subject + topicalizer + complementizer-clause marker + dative + prepositional phrase + verb. "я" = I (subject); "вот" is a discourse filler meaning "now/well"; "что" here is a complementizer introducing what he'll tell; "тебе" is dative 'to you' (I will tell you); "про это" = 'about this'; "скажу" is perfective future 1sg (I will say/tell) indicating a single forthcoming action.
segments.p2-7-g26: "про теперешнее-то твое житье": "про" = 'about'; "теперешнее" (neuter) = 'present/current' modifying "житье"; the enclitic "-то" attached to "теперешнее" is emphatic ("the very present"); "твое" agrees in gender/number with "житье" (neuter) = 'your'; "житье" (here colloquial/literary) = 'way of life/living'. The colon signals elaboration.
segments.p2-7-g27: "вот ты теперь хоть и молодая," — "вот" is a filler ('now/well/so'); "ты" subject; "теперь" = 'now'; "хоть и" is a concessive phrase meaning 'even though' / 'though' (here conceding youth while introducing contrast); "молодая" is feminine nominative adjective agreeing with "ты".
segments.p2-7-g28: Single adjective in a list of qualities: "пригожая" (literary/colloquial) = 'comely/pretty' modifying the understood subject "ты"; it's feminine nominative to agree with "ты."
segments.p2-7-g29: "хорошая" continues the enumeration of qualities (feminine nominative) = 'good/nice' describing the subject.
segments.p2-7-g30: "с душой" = 'with a soul' ("с" + instrumental). Here "душой" (instr.) marks a characteristic: soulful, heartfelt; it's an idiomatic quality phrase.
segments.p2-7-g31: "с чувством" likewise uses "с" + instrumental ("чувством") to mean 'with feeling' or 'sensitive' — another evaluative attribute in the same list. The semicolon groups the qualities before the following contrast.
segments.p2-7-g32: "ну" is an interjection/transition ('well') used to change register or prompt the next question; it's colloquial and marks a slight pause.
segments.p2-7-g33: "а знаешь ли ты," — "а" is contrastive/introducing a question; "знаешь" is 2sg present 'do you know'; the enclitic "ли" turns it into an indirect yes/no question; "ты" is the emphatic subject (optional).
segments.p2-7-g34: "что вот я," begins the subordinate content clause: "что" = 'that' (complementizer); "вот" adds immediacy ('well/now'); "я" = I (subject of the embedded clause).
segments.p2-7-g35: "как только давеча очнулся," — "как только" is the temporal conjunction 'as soon as'; "давеча" is a colloquial/literary adverb 'recently/the other day'; "очнулся" is past tense (perfective) 'came to/regained consciousness' (masculine singular), describing a completed event just prior to the reaction.
segments.p2-7-g36: Impersonal construction: "мне (dat.) тотчас и гадко стало (neuter past) быть здесь с тобой!" — literally 'to me it immediately became disgusting to be here with you.' "мне" marks the experiencer; "тотчас" = immediately; "гадко" is a predicative adverb 'feeling disgusted/sick'; "стало" (neuter past of 'стать') forms the impersonal "it became"; the infinitive "быть" is the complement ('to be here with you'); "с тобой" instrumental = 'with you.'
segments.p2-7-g37: Entire sentence is impersonal modal: "Только" restricts ('only'); "в пьяном виде" is a noun phrase meaning 'in a drunken state' ("в" + prepositional/locative phrase with "виде" = 'in the form/state of'); "ведь" is a particle 'after all/you know' adding emphasis; "и" here is an additional intensifier ('even/indeed'); "можно" is impersonal modal 'it is possible/can (one)'; "сюда" = 'to here'; "попасть" is infinitive 'to get in/to get to' and is governed by "можно."
segments.p2-7-g38: "А будь ты в другом месте," uses the subjunctive/imperative form "будь" (from 'быть') to form a counterfactual conditional: literally 'be you in another place' = 'if you were somewhere else.' "А" is contrastive/introductory; "в другом месте" states the hypothetical location.
segments.p2-7-g39: "живи" is the 2sg imperative of "жить" ('live'); here it is an instruction or suggestion addressed to the addressee within the hypothetical—"live (your life)"—and links to the following clause describing how to live.
segments.p2-7-g40: "как добрые люди живут," — "как" = 'as/like'; "добрые люди" = 'good/decent people' (nominative plural); "живут" = 3pl present 'live.' This clause gives the manner: 'live as good people live.'
segments.p2-7-g41: "так я," — "так" = 'then/in that case' (linking the hypothetical life to the speaker's possible reaction); "я" = I (subject) introduces the speaker's conditional response.
segments.p2-7-g42: "может быть," is the modal phrase 'perhaps/maybe' ("может" + infinitive "быть" colloquially fused), hedging the following conditional statements about what the speaker might do.
segments.p2-7-g43: "не то что волочился б за тобой," — "не то что" introduces a contrast 'not that (I would...)'; "волочился" is past imperfective of "волочиться" ('to trail/drag oneself along, to hang about'); the enclitic "б" is the conditional particle ('would'); "за тобой" = 'after/behind you' (following you). Together: 'not that I would have trailed after you.'
segments.p2-7-g44: "а просто влюбился б в тебя," — "а" contrastive 'but'; "просто" = 'simply/merely'; "влюбился" past perfective 'fell in love'; "б" conditional 'would'; "в тебя" accusative 'in you' = 'in love with you.' It states the alternative reaction the speaker might have had.
segments.p2-7-g45: "рад бы взгляду был твоему," — somewhat inverted order: "рад" ('glad') is a predicative that takes a dative; "бы" is the conditional marker; "взгляду" is dative 'to/for the look' (meaning 'at your glance'); "был" is past of 'be' used with the conditional particle. Word order emphasizes "взгляду"; "твоему" agrees with "взгляду."
segments.p2-7-g46: "не то что слову;" — again "не то что" = 'not that (I would care for)'; "слову" is dative (or sometimes used adverbially) meaning 'for your word/words' (i.e. his preference would be for her look, not her words). The semicolon separates parallel contrasts.
segments.p2-7-g47: "у ворот бы тебя подстерегал," — "у ворот" ('by/at the gates') uses "у" + genitive; "бы" conditional particle; "тебя" = you (accusative, object of "подстерегал"); "подстерегал" past imperfective 'lay in wait for,' so the whole phrase means 'I would lie in wait for you at the gate.'
segments.p2-7-g48: "на коленках бы перед тобой выстаивал;" — "на коленках" = 'on (my) knees' (prepositional/locative phrase); "бы" conditional; "перед тобой" = 'in front of you' ("перед" + instrumental); "выстаивал" is past imperfective 'would stand/wait (by standing),' giving a vivid image of penitent waiting. The clause uses typical conditional morphology.
segments.p2-7-g49: "как на невесту б свою на тебя смотрел," — word order is marked but parseable: "как" = 'as/like'; "на невесту" (accusative) = 'as (if) at a bride'; "б" = conditional; "свою" = 'my own' agreeing with "невесту"; "на тебя смотрел" = 'would look at you' (literal order places 'на тебя' before the verb). The clause means 'I would look at you as if you were my bride.'
segments.p2-7-g50: "да еще за честь почитал." — "да еще" = 'and moreover / even'; "за честь" ("за" + accusative) = 'as an honor' / 'for an honor'; "почитал" past of "почитать" meaning 'regard/respect' — together: 'and would even have considered it an honor.' "да" here is the archaic/poetic conjunction 'and.'
segments.p2-7-g51: "Подумать про тебя что-нибудь нечистое не осмелился бы." — infinitive "Подумать" begins a negated conditional construction; "про тебя" = 'about you'; "что-нибудь" = 'anything' (indefinite); "нечистое" (neuter to agree with "что-нибудь") = 'unclean/impure'; "не осмелился бы" = past perfective + conditional particle ('would not have dared'). The overall order (infinitive first) is stylistic and yields: 'I would not have dared to think anything impure about you.'
segments.p2-7-g319: "В могиле" is a prepositional phrase (в + prepositional могиле) meaning "in the grave." The noun phrase that follows, "слякоть," is placed after the locative for an existential, verbless construction (English "there is"); Russian often omits быть, so the list of items is presented as present existence: "In the grave — slush."
segments.p2-7-g320: A single noun in apposition to the previous item in the list. Here "мразь" is used as a coarse noun meaning "filth/scum," continuing the catalogue of unpleasant things in the grave.
segments.p2-7-g321: "снег мокрый" is a noun + adjective phrase meaning "wet snow." Placing the adjective after the noun (more marked than the neutral "мокрый снег") gives a terse, enumerative, slightly colloquial or emphatic tone in this list.
segments.p2-7-g322: This is a short rhetorical question. "Не для тебя же церемониться?" literally: "Not to be ceremonious for you?" — with "для + genitive (для тебя)" meaning "for you," "же" an emphatic particle ("after all/precisely"), and the verb "церемониться" in the infinitive meaning "to observe formalities/behave ceremoniously." The negative question implies: "there's no need to be ceremonious toward you, is there?"
segments.p2-7-g323: "Спущай-ка" is a colloquial imperative of "спустить" with the softening/emphatic particle "-ка" ("do so for me/just do it"). It commands someone to lower or let something/someone down; "ее" is the accusative object "her/it." Together: "Let her down (now)."
segments.p2-7-g324: A vocative address — the speaker calls someone by name (diminutive/nickname). Grammatically it's direct address (no case marking beyond the form).
segments.p2-7-g325: "Ишь" is an interjection (roughly "well, look/just see") used to draw attention or express mild scorn; "ведь" is a particle meaning "after all" or reinforcing the comment. Together they introduce a derisive remark: "just look, after all..."
segments.p2-7-g326: A punctuation-only segment (comma) marking a pause; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.p2-7-g327: "учась" is a gerund/short participial form from "учиться" meaning "while (she was) learning" or ironically "'learning'" (quoted in speech). "и тут" = "and here/at that moment." "верх ногами пошла" is an idiomatic description: "верх" (top/upper part) with "ногами" (instrumental plural of "нога") yields the adverbial image "with the top by the feet," i.e. "upside down/top over heels"; "пошла" is past-feminine of "пойти" used here to mean "went/turned/fell (in that manner)." The whole clause: "and here, while 'learning,' she went over upside down."
segments.p2-7-g328: A single-word label/name. "таковская" is an adjectival/onomastic form (a surname or family-name form) used to identify the woman — here likely the surname "Takovskaya." Grammatically it's nominative and functions as an appositive label.
segments.p2-7-g329: Укороти is an imperative singular (perfective укоротить) telling someone to 'shorten' or 'make shorter'. веревки is the (accusative) plural of верёвка 'ropes' (form identical to nominative plural) and is the direct object. The enclitic particle то attached after веревки (веревки-то) is colloquial and adds emphasis or a slightly brusque/insistent tone ('do shorten the ropes, will you' / 'shorten the ropes, then').
segments.p2-7-g330: пострел here functions as an address/nickname ('shooter') — a nominal form used to call or refer to a person, not the verb 'to shoot'. The trailing punctuation/quote mark reflects the original speech marking; grammatically it's a vocative-like use of the noun.
segments.p2-7-g331: Ладно is an interjection/predicative meaning 'all right' or 'fine'; и is a particle here, roughly 'even' or 'also', used with так to form the idiom 'и так' meaning 'as it is' or 'like that'; так is an adverb meaning 'so/that way' — together Ладно и так expresses acceptance: 'it's fine like that' or 'that will do.'
segments.p2-7-g360: Я жила is the past tense, feminine singular of жить ('I lived'); the dash marks an abrupt break to the following clause. жизни не видала places жизни (genitive) before the verb for emphasis with negation: видала is a colloquial past form of видеть/видать ('to see/know'), so жизни не видала means literally 'hadn't seen life' i.e. 'had never known (what) life is.'
segments.p2-7-g361: моя жизнь is the nominative subject 'my life.' на обтирку пошла uses на + noun of purpose ('for wiping') and пошла (past feminine of пойти) in an idiomatic sense 'went to/become used as,' so the clause means 'my life was put to/used as a wiping cloth.' The verb agrees with жизнь (feminine).
segments.p2-7-g362: ее is the accusative pronoun 'it/her' (referring back to 'my life') placed up front for emphasis. в кабаке на Сенной is a locative/prepositional phrase ('in the tavern on the Sennaya'), with Сенной functioning as an adjective form of the place name. пропили is past plural of пропить ('drink away'), so the implied 'they' drank it away there.
segments.p2-7-g363: пустите is the imperative plural (or polite singular) of пустить 'let/allow' and serves as a direct plea to the addressees; the comma marks the vocative that follows.
segments.p2-7-g364: добрые люди is a direct address (no special vocative case in Russian): добрые is the adjective 'kind' modifying люди 'people.' The comma shows these words are being addressed by the speaker.
segments.p2-7-g365: еще раз is an adverbial phrase 'once more/again.' на свете ('in the world') uses the prepositional form свет + archaic locative-style на to mean 'in the world.' пожить is the perfective/short-duration infinitive 'to live (for a while)' and follows the imperative пустите to form the request 'let (me) live once more.'
segments.p2-7-g368: Вошел в пафос is a figurative perfective past 'entered into pathos' — 'became overwrought/affected.' До того is part of the degree phrase до того, что 'to such an extent that'; пафос here means rhetorical pathos/pomp (the noun is accusative after в).
segments.p2-7-g369: Что introduces the result clause 'that...'. У меня expresses a possessive experiencer construction ('I had / there was to me'), самого is an emphasizer 'my very own.' Горловая спазма is a colloquial/uneven phrasing meaning 'a throat spasm' (горловая = 'throat-'), and приготовлялась is imperfective past 'was preparing / was getting ready' (reflexive form meaning coming on).
