segments.p2-7-g572: This phrase contains two coordinated past imperfective actions. 'она хранила' (past imperfective) expresses habitual or sustained action ('she kept/cherished'); 'письмо этого студента' is the direct object with 'этого студента' in the genitive modifying 'письмо'. 'как драгоценность' is a simile ('as a treasure'). 'и сбегала за этой единственной своей драгоценностью' — 'сбегала' (imperfective past) indicates repeated short runs; 'за' + instrumental ('этой ... драгоценностью') means 'to run off to fetch/after' that thing; 'своей' is the reflexive possessive referring back to 'она' (her own).
segments.p2-7-g573: 'не желая' is a perfective/imperative gerund form (деепричастие) with the negative particle 'не' and expresses an accompanying attitude: 'not wishing' or 'not wanting'. It introduces the purpose/result clause that follows.
segments.p2-7-g574: 'чтоб' (colloquial variant of 'чтобы') introduces the subordinate clause expressing the undesired outcome; with a past-tense verb it functions like a subjunctive ('that I should/could leave'). 'я' is the subject, 'ушел' is the past form of the perfective 'уйти' used here in a subordinate clause to mark the wished-for/feared event ('leave').
segments.p2-7-g575: 'не узнав' is a perfective gerund (деепричастие) meaning 'without having learned/without finding out'. 'о том' ('about that') points forward to the content given by the following 'что'-clauses.
segments.p2-7-g576: Subordinate content clause introduced by 'что' ('that'). 'и' here has the emphatic/also sense ('too'); 'ее' is the accusative/object pronoun 'her'; 'любят' is 3rd-person plural present ('they love' or 'people love'); 'честно и искренно' are adverbs modifying the verb ('honestly and sincerely').
segments.p2-7-g577: Another 'что'-clause giving further content: 'что и с ней говорят почтительно' — 'that people also speak with her respectfully.' 'с' requires the pronoun form 'ней' after a preposition; 'говорят' is 3rd-person plural present; 'почтительно' is an adverb ('respectfully').
segments.p2-7-g578: Наверно (colloquial variant of 'наверное') is a sentence adverb meaning 'probably' or 'most likely', setting the speaker's degree of certainty.
segments.p2-7-g579: Impersonal construction where 'этому письму' is dative (the thing to which fate pertains) and 'суждено было пролежать' ('was destined to lie') combines the short predicate 'суждено' with past 'было' plus an infinitive 'пролежать' indicating the fate/expected result. 'так и' is an emphatic particle combination ('just so/indeed'), 'в шкатулке' is locative, and 'без последствий' uses genitive plural to mean 'without consequences.'
segments.p2-7-g580: Но всё равно is a colloquial connective meaning 'but all the same' or 'nevertheless'; it signals contrast or dismissal of a possible objection. Punctuation here (semicolon) separates the remark from the following assertion.
segments.p2-7-g581: 'я уверен' is a simple predicative clause: the pronoun 'я' (I) and the short-form adjective 'уверен' meaning 'am sure' or 'am certain'. The short-form adjective functions as the predicate.
segments.p2-7-g582: 'что' introduces a subordinate clause. 'всю жизнь' ('all her life') is an adverbial accusative/time expression; 'хранила бы' is past tense plus the conditional particle 'бы', forming a counterfactual/hypothetical ('would have kept'); 'его' is the object pronoun referring back to the letter (here 'it'); 'как драгоценность' states the manner ('as a treasure').
segments.p2-7-g583: 'как гордость свою и свое оправдание' enumerates parallel noun phrases introduced by 'как' ('as'). 'свою' and 'свое' are reflexive possessives agreeing in gender/number with the nouns they modify (гордость — feminine, оправдание — neuter) and refer back to the subject ('she').
segments.p2-7-g584: 'и вот теперь' is a discourse phrase ('and now at this moment'); 'сама' emphasizes that she herself acted; 'в такую минуту' ('at such a moment') is an accusative time phrase; 'вспомнила и принесла' are two perfective past verbs ('remembered and brought') indicating completed sequential actions; 'это письмо' is the direct object.
segments.p2-7-g585: 'чтоб' introduces purpose ('in order that'); here it controls the infinitival notion expressed by the reflexive verb 'погордиться' (perfective, 'to take pride'). 'наивно' modifies the verb ('naively'), and 'передо мной' ('before me') uses 'перед' in the form 'передо' plus the instrumental 'мной' to mean 'in my presence' or 'in front of me.'
segments.p2-7-g586: 'восстановить себя' is an infinitive with reflexive 'себя' meaning 'to restore herself'; 'в моих глазах' is a fixed locution ('in my eyes') meaning 'in my estimation' or 'in my view.' This is another stated purpose/result in the chain of intentions.
segments.p2-7-g587: Again 'чтоб' marks purpose: 'чтоб и я видел' — literally 'so that I, too, would see.' Russian commonly uses a past-tense form ('видел') in such subordinate clauses to express desired or expected perception (subjunctive-like meaning). 'и' = 'also/even.'
segments.p2-7-g588: Same construction: 'чтоб и я похвалил' — 'so that I, too, would praise.' The past form 'похвалил' after 'чтоб' carries the speaker's desired/resulting meaning (subjunctive nuance).
segments.p2-7-g589: 'Я ничего не сказал' is a simple past-clause with an explicit subject 'я'. 'ничего' (nothing) combines with the negative particle 'не' to give the negation 'said nothing' (standard Russian negation uses both).
segments.p2-7-g590: 'пожал ей руку' — 'пожал' (past of 'пожать') here means 'shook' (hands); 'ей' is the dative 'to her'; 'руку' is the direct object in accusative. 'и вышел' is a simple past action 'and (I) went out/left.' The actions are presented as a sequence.

sentences.p2-7-s1: Oh, come now, Liza — what book is there when I myself feel disgusted and out of place?
sentences.p2-7-s2: Besides — it's not that I'm a stranger.
sentences.p2-7-s3: All of this has now awakened in my soul.
sentences.p2-7-s6: Really — really, don't you yourself find it disgusting here?
sentences.p2-7-s17: And here, you know, I know that I only have to whistle, and you, whether you want to or not, will follow me, and it will no longer be I who answers to your will, but you to mine.
sentences.p2-7-s18: Even the humblest peasant will hire himself out as a worker, and still will not sell off his whole self, and he even knows that he has a term.
sentences.p2-7-s20: Just think: what are you giving up here?
sentences.p2-7-s34: How could he love (you) if he knows that you will be called away from him this very instant?
sentences.p2-7-s35: What a mischief-maker he is after that!
sentences.p2-7-s36: Does he respect you even a bit?
sentences.p2-7-s22: You enslave the soul — the soul in which you have no power — together with the body!
sentences.p2-7-s23: You surrender your love to be mocked by every drunkard!
sentences.p2-7-s37: What do you have in common with him?
sentences.p2-7-s38: He laughs at you and even robs you — that's all his love!
sentences.p2-7-s39: It's a good thing he doesn't beat you.
sentences.p2-7-s29: After all, there's nothing more hurtful to a girl than being offended, do you understand?
sentences.p2-7-s30: Well, I've heard they flatter you, you fools — they even let you have lovers here.
sentences.p2-7-s31: But it's all just a bit of play, a deception, a laughter at you, and yet you believe.
sentences.p2-7-s32: What, really, does he, the lover, love you?
sentences.p2-7-s7: No — apparently habit matters a great deal!
sentences.p2-7-s8: The devil knows what habit can make of a person.
sentences.p2-7-s9: Do you really — seriously — think that you'll never grow old, that you'll always be pretty, and that they will keep you here for all eternity?
sentences.p2-7-s10: I'm not even speaking of the fact that it's nasty here.
sentences.p2-7-s25: — After all, that's everything; after all, it's a diamond, a maiden's treasure — love itself!
sentences.p2-7-s26: After all, to deserve this love, some are ready to lay down their soul, to go to death.
sentences.p2-7-s27: But what is your love worth now?
sentences.p2-7-s28: You're completely bought, wholly, and why bother trying to win love here when everything is possible even without love.
sentences.p2-7-s41: Ask him, if you have such a one: will he marry you?
sentences.p2-7-s42: Why, he'll burst out laughing in your face, unless he merely spits on you or knocks you down — and to himself, maybe his whole worth is only two broken groschen.
sentences.p2-7-s43: And for what, you might think, have you ruined your life here?
sentences.p2-7-s44: What — they give you coffee to drink and feed you well?
sentences.p2-7-s77: Consumption is that kind of disease; it is not a fever.
sentences.p2-7-s78: Here, right up to the last minute, a man hopes and says that he is healthy.
sentences.p2-7-s80: And it's even profitable for the landlady.
sentences.p2-7-s49: All of this, you know, will fly off on the mail coaches.
sentences.p2-7-s51: And they won't just push you out — long before that they'll begin to pick at you, begin to reproach you, begin to scold you, as if you hadn't given her your health, as if you hadn't ruined your youth and soul for her in vain, but as if you, of all people, had ruined her, set her adrift in the world, robbed her.
sentences.p2-7-s52: And don't expect support: your other girlfriends will also attack you to ingratiate themselves with her, because here everyone is in slavery; conscience and pity have long been lost.
sentences.p2-7-s45: But what's the point of feeding you, then?
sentences.p2-7-s46: Another—an honest one—such a morsel wouldn't have gone down her throat, because she knows why she is fed.
sentences.p2-7-s47: Here you are obliged; well, you'll remain obliged — and to the very end of ends you'll remain obliged, until the very moment when the guests begin to disdain you.
sentences.p2-7-s48: And this will come soon; don't rely on youth.
sentences.p2-7-s53: They have sunk low, and even worse, meaner, more hurtful than these insults — nothing on earth is more so.
sentences.p2-7-s54: And you'll lay down everything here — everything, without a will: your health, your youth, your beauty, your hopes, and at twenty‑two you'll look like a thirty‑five‑year‑old; and that's still fortunate, if you're not ill — pray to God for that.
sentences.p2-7-s55: After all, you probably think now that you have no work — just revelry!
sentences.p2-7-s56: Indeed, there is no work in the world heavier and more like penal labour, and there never has been.
sentences.p2-7-s57: One heart, it seems, would have completely poured out in tears.
sentences.p2-7-s58: And you won't dare say a word, not even half a word; when they drive you out of here, you'll go along like someone guilty.
sentences.p2-7-s59: You'll move to another place, then to a third, then somewhere else, and finally you'll get to Sennaya.
sentences.p2-7-s60: And there they'll already begin to beat you in passing; that's the local courtesy; there a host can't even caress without having first beaten [someone].
sentences.p2-7-s61: You don't believe it's so unpleasant there, do you?
sentences.p2-7-s62: Go on—have a look sometime; maybe you'll see it with your own eyes.
sentences.p2-7-s63: I once saw one there on New Year's, standing at the door.
sentences.p2-7-s64: They shoved her out to mock her — their own people essentially left her to get a little frostbitten for crying so much, and they shut the door behind her.
sentences.p2-7-s69: Don't you believe that you'll be the same?
sentences.p2-7-s70: And I would not like to believe it, but how do you know — maybe ten, eight years ago that very same woman, with her salted fish, arrived here from somewhere, fresh as a little cherub, innocent, tidy; she knew no evil and blushed at every word.
sentences.p2-7-s71: Perhaps she was like you — proud, touchy, unlike other people, she looked like a princess and herself knew that complete happiness awaited whoever might love her and whoever she might love.
sentences.p2-7-s65: By nine o'clock in the morning she was already completely drunk, dishevelled, half-naked, and all beaten up.
sentences.p2-7-s66: She had powdered herself, but her eyes were rimmed with kohl; blood was running from her nose and from her teeth — some cabman had only just fixed her up.
sentences.p2-7-s67: She sat down on the stone steps, with some salted fish in her hands; she wailed, lamenting something about her 'учась', and was striking the stair treads with the fish.
sentences.p2-7-s68: And by the porch cabmen and drunken soldiers had crowded together and were taunting her.
sentences.p2-7-s94: Just look — he's lying on his side.
sentences.p2-7-s95: Was he a man too, or not?
sentences.p2-7-s96: Well, all right, go to sleep.
sentences.p2-7-s13: Anyway, I'll tell you this about it — about your present life: you're now young, comely, good, soulful, sensitive; well, do you know that as soon as I came to myself the other day I immediately felt sick at being here with you!
sentences.p2-7-s14: You can only get in here in a drunken state.
sentences.p2-7-s15: But if you were in another place, live as good people live — then I, perhaps, would not, rather than trail after you, simply have fallen in love with you; I would have been glad of your look, not of your word; I would have waited for you by the gate, would have stood on my knees before you; would have looked at you as at my bride, and even counted it an honor.
sentences.p2-7-s16: I wouldn't have dared to think anything unclean about you.
sentences.p2-7-s89: In the grave there's slush, filth, wet snow — it's not as if one should be ceremonious for you, is it?
sentences.p2-7-s90: "Let her down, Vanyukha; look at that — 'learning,' and here she went top over heels, the Takovskaya."
sentences.p2-7-s103: I lived — I never knew life; my life was used as a wiping cloth; they drank it away in the tavern on the Sennaya; let me, kind people, live once more in the world!
sentences.p2-7-s106: I got so carried away with pathos that even my own throat was getting ready to spasm, and...
sentences.p2-7-s109: Suddenly I stopped, rose a little in alarm, and, tilting my head timidly, with a beating heart began to listen.
sentences.p2-7-s110: There was even reason to be embarrassed.
sentences.p2-7-s73: And what if, at that very moment when she was beating those dirty steps with that fish, drunk and dishevelled, what if at that moment she recalled all her former, pure years in her father's house — when she still went to school and the neighbour's son lay in wait for her on the road, assuring her that he would love her all his life, that he would lay his fate at her feet, and when together they vowed to love one another forever and to be married, just wait till they grow up!
sentences.p2-7-s74: No, Liza — happiness, happiness to you if, somewhere there, in a corner, in a cellar, like the one from the other day, you should sooner die of consumption.

sentences.p2-7-s117: But now, having achieved the effect, I suddenly chickened out.
sentences.p2-7-s118: No — never, never before have I been a witness to such despair!
sentences.p2-7-s119: She lay face down, burying her face firmly in the pillow and clasping it with both hands.
sentences.p2-7-s120: Her chest was being torn (she felt her chest torn).
sentences.p2-7-s81: Don't worry, that's so; she has, you see, sold her soul, and on top of that owes money, so you can't even utter a peep.
